questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced
questions.q: A particular hydrogen like atom has its ground state binding "energy 122.4 eV . Its is in ground state. Then:
options: Its atomic number is 3
An electron of 90 eV can excite it.
An electron of kinetic energy nearly 91.8 eV can be brought to almost rest by this atom.
An electron of kinetic energy 2.6 eV may emerge from the atom when electron of kinetic energy 125 eV collides with this atom.
questions.source: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced
questions.q: Energy liberated in the de-excitation of hydrogen atom from $3^{\text {rd }}$ level to $1^{\text {st }}$ level falls on a photocathode. Later when the same photo-cathode is exposed to a spectrum of some unknown hydrogen like gas, excited to $2^{\text {nd }}$ energy level, it is found that the de-Broglie wavelength of the fastest photoelectrons, now ejected has decreased by a factor of 3 . For this new gas, difference of energies of $2^{\text {nd }}$ Lyman line and $1^{\text {st }}$ Balmer line is found to be 3 times the ionization potential of the hydrogen atom. Select the correct statement(s) :
options: The gas is lithium.
The gas is helium.
The work function of photo-cathode is 8.5 eV .
The work function of photo-cathode is 5.5 eV .
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & E_0 z^2\left(1-\frac{1}{9}\right)-E_0 z^2\left(\frac{1}{4}-\frac{1}{9}\right)=3 E_0 \\ & Z=2 \\ & \lambda_1 / \lambda_2=3 \\ & \mathrm{KE}_1=E_0\left(1-\frac{1}{9}\right)-\phi \\ & K_2=E_0 z^2\left(1-\frac{1}{4}\right)-\phi \\ & K E \propto \frac{1}{\lambda^2}=8.5 \mathrm{eV} .\end{aligned}$
questions.source: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced
questions.q:
Suppose the potential energy between electron and proton at a distance r is given by $\frac{-\mathrm{ke}^2}{3 \mathrm{r}^3}$.
Application of Bohr's theory to hydrogen atom in this case shows that
options: Energy in the $n$th orbit is proportional to $\mathrm{n}^6$
Energy is proportional to $\mathrm{m}^{-3}$.(m: mass of electron)
Energy of the $n$th orbit is proportional to $n^{-2}$
Energy is proportional to $\mathrm{m}^3$ ( $\mathrm{m}=$ mass of electron)
questions.solution:
$|F|=\frac{d U}{d r}=\frac{k e^2}{r^4}$
$F=\frac{m v^2}{r}$
$\Rightarrow \frac{k^2}{r^4}=\frac{m v^2}{r} \ldots(1)$
and $\mathrm{mvr}=\frac{\mathrm{nh}}{2 \pi} \quad \ldots(2)$
From (1) and (2) $r=\frac{k^2 4 \pi^2}{n^2} \frac{m}{n^2}=\frac{A m}{n^2}$
$T . E=\frac{1}{2} P . E=\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{k^2}{3\left(\frac{\mathrm{Am}}{\mathrm{n}^2}\right)^3}$
T.E. $\propto \mathrm{n}^6, \mathrm{~T} . \mathrm{E} . \propto \mathrm{m}^{-3}$
questions.source: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced
questions.q: The energy, the magnitude of linear momentum and orbital radius of an electron in a hydrogen atom corresponding to the quantum number $n$ are $\mathrm{E}, \mathrm{p}$ and $r$ respectively. Then according to Bohr's theory of hydrogen atom
options: Epr is proportional to $\frac{1}{n}$
$\frac{\mathrm{p}}{\mathrm{E}}$ is proportional to n
Er is constant for all orbits
pr is proportional to n
questions.source: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced
questions.q: Hydrogen atoms absorbs radiation of wavelength $\lambda_0$ and consequently emit radiations of 6 different wavelengths of which three wavelengths are shorter than $\lambda_0$.
options: The final excited state of the atoms is $n=4$
The initial state of the atoms may be $\mathrm{n}=2$
The initial state of the atoms may be $n=3$
There are three transitions belonging to Lyman series.
questions.solution: $\frac{n(n-1)}{2}=6 \Rightarrow n=4$ If the initial state were $n=3$, in the emission spectrum, no wavelengths shorter than $\lambda_0$ would have occurred. This is possible if initial state were $\mathrm{n}=2$ $\therefore \quad(A),(B)$ and $(D)$
questions.source: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced
